selector.exact: What has inhabited the earth since the Ice Age is man

annotations.anchor: What has inhabited the earth since the Ice Age is man, not 'peoples.'
annotations.note: The chapter sets out 'to demolish' a Romantic idea: 'the People' as a primordial, fixed unit out of which history is made — the 'Nation' of 1789, the 'Volk' of 1813. For Spengler this is a back-projection. The real, separable realities are two — race (the blood, rooted in a landscape) and language (the form of waking-consciousness) — and they have wholly independent histories. 'Peoples' are something later and made, not the bedrock; he untangles them here so he can redefine 'people' and 'nation' in the next chapter.

selector.exact: of Being, is invested with a character of duration. This I call race.

annotations.anchor: the cosmic-plantlike side of life, of Being … This I call race.
annotations.note: Spengler's own definition, and it is crucial for reading the rest of the volume: 'race' is 'the blood which circles, carried on by procreation, in a narrow or a wide landscape' — the duration of the cosmic, Being side of life, bound to a soil. It belongs with time, destiny, and yearning, where language belongs with space and fear. This is a metaphysical, not a biological, concept — as the next notes make emphatic.

selector.exact: has travelled as flesh over the fixed skeleton-form of the land

annotations.anchor: 'race' has travelled as flesh over the fixed skeleton-form of the land
annotations.note: The load-bearing point of the chapter, and one a modern reader must hold firmly. Spengler's 'race' is NOT the biological race of his era's anthropology — the bulk of the chapter demolishes skull-measuring race-science (Blumenbach, Huxley, the very idea of an 'Aryan skull'). 'As soon as light is let through it' — in an X-ray — '"race" vanishes.' For Spengler race is a living quality of flesh, movement, and above all land: bone-structure is 'a growth of the landscape and never a function of the blood,' so 'race has travelled as flesh over the fixed skeleton-form of the land.' This matters historically: when the Nazis later seized his words 'blood' and 'race,' they meant the biological racism Spengler here attacks — he ridiculed Aryan skull-science, quarrelled with the Nazi race-theorist Rosenberg, and saw his 1933 book suppressed by the regime. But do not mistake him for a liberal or a tidy anti-Nazi: he voted for Hitler in 1932, was an authoritarian nationalist, and carried his own civilizational racism (the 'Coloured World-Revolution' he dreaded in The Hour of Decision). His quarrel was with race-biology, not with racial hierarchy as such. So read his 'blood' here as metaphysics (Being, destiny, soil-rootedness) rather than descent — but read the man with both eyes open.

The clearest proof that Spengler's 'race' is made, not inherited. A nobility or a war-band, standing 'spiritually firm and united in the presence of its Destiny,' forms a bodily ideal and so 'breeds' a race — 'French noblesse and Prussian Landadel are genuine race-denotations,' and the same shared destiny 'bred the types of the European Jew … with his thousand years of ghetto life.' Race here is forged by comradeship and a 'race-ideal,' independent of any choosing of wives — the opposite of a doctrine of fixed descent.

selector.exact: Blumenbach classified the races of man according to skull-forms

annotations.anchor: Blumenbach … skull-forms; Müller … hair; Topinard … skin-colour; Huxley …
annotations.note: Spengler skewers the race-science of his day as nationally self-serving guesswork: 'Blumenbach classified the races of man according to skull-forms, Friedrich Müller (as a true German) by hair and language-structure, Topinard (as a true Frenchman) by skin-colour and shape of nose, and Huxley (as a true Englishman) by … sport characteristics' — descriptions 'as worthless as the descriptions of "wanted" men.' The 'soulless causal concatenation of superficial characters' is exactly the materialist method he rejects everywhere; here it happens to land on the anthropometry that the following decade would weaponize.

selector.exact: Race is something cosmic and psychic

annotations.anchor: Race is something cosmic and psychic … Languages, on the other hand, are causal forms
annotations.note: The structural pair behind the chapter's title 'Peoples, Races, Tongues.' Race and language are two independent realities with two independent histories: race is cosmic and psychic — the Being side, periodic, blood-deep, belonging to time and destiny; language is a 'causal form' — the waking-being side, belonging to space and communication. A man is at once 'derivative of a blood-unit' and 'member of an understanding-unit,' and the two need never coincide. Confusing them, Spengler says, is exactly the error baked into the word 'peoples.'

selector.exact: There are, therefore, expression-languages and communication-languages.

annotations.note: A distinction worth carrying out of this chapter. An 'expression-language' merely displays a presence — 'the longing inherent in all life to actualize itself before witnesses' — and assumes only an 'I'; a 'communication-language' is addressed to a 'thou' and needs a shared sense of meanings. Art and religion live mostly in the first, everyday speech in the second. Spengler's lovely corollary: 'it is by some "thou" that we first came to the knowledge of an "I,"' so that the self is born from address — 'a designation for the fact that a bridge exists to some other being.'
